title: Inna and its Affects on Sentence Structure, Mubtada' and Khabar
summary: This lesson focuses on the grammatical effects of using 'inna' at the beginning of a sentence, particularly how it impacts the case endings (harakat) of subsequent nouns. Students learn that 'inna' initiates a declaratory sentence structure where the mubtada' (subject) takes a fatha, and the khabar (predicate) maintains its default case. The teacher provides examples with proper nouns and attributes, emphasizing when tanween is applied versus using 'al'.
key_points: Using "inna" at the start of a sentence requires the mubtada' (subject noun) to take a fatha (ال Dundun). For example, "inna al-darsa sa'ban" instead of "ad-daru sa'ban".
If the following word has 'al', it will not get tanween. Exceptions include names and ghayr munsarif terms which do not take kasra or tanween (e.g., inna aminata talibatun).
The khabar (predicate) retains its original case unless specified otherwise, such as remaining dhamma for masculine singular nouns.
"inna" affects all parts of the sentence structure but only changes the mubtada'; other components must align with grammatical rules like corresponding harakat in musaf-sifa pairs.
questions: [00:20:00] Q: What is 'azzabun' and why did Sheikh mention scholars staying unmarried? — A: "Azzabun" means unmarried. The sheikh cited scholars who dedicated themselves to knowledge, contrasting with those who marry.
